title: Finding definite integrals using area formulas
description: Practice evaluating definite integrals by finding the area using shapes (like rectangles & circles) under a function.
topic_path: Finding definite integrals using area formulas

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $g$ is graphed.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 5, negative 4), moves upward to (negative 3, negative 1), continues upward through (negative 2, 0) to (1, 2), moves downward to (3, 1), continues downward and ends at (5, negative 2). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b7aa716fc293f73deed028250c438da623144ae3)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{3} g(x) \,dx$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{3} g(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{3} g(x) \,dx$ is the area between the curve $y=g(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-2$ and $x=3$.

![The graph of function g has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = negative 2 and x = 3 shaded. The segment at the top of the shaded region starts at (negative 2, 0), moves upward to (1, 2), then moves downward to (3, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/86c22cf00aac20c8c1b326f92b41deaa46e99f8a)

Since the curve is $\blueD{\text{above}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the area is $\blueD{\text{positive}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function g has the shaded region divided into 2 triangles and a rectangle. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bfec1fdbc3defb87ca2c259a78ce4a9a02e7e51f)

We have one triangle with base $3$ and height $2$, one triangle with base $2$ and height $1$, and one rectangle with base $2$ and height $1$:

$\underbrace{\dfrac{3\cdot 2}{2}}_{\text{triangle #1}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{2\cdot 1}{2}}_{\text{triangle #2}}+\underbrace{2\cdot 1}_{\text{rectangle}}=6$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{3} g(x) \,dx=6$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $h$ is graphed.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 5, negative 3), moves downward to (negative 4, negative 5), moves upward to (negative 1, 1), continues upward to (0, 2), moves horizontally to the right to (2, 2), moves upward through (4, 3) and ends at about (5, 3.5). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8a127902db77df63eb0548ab9c40469ae2924049)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{4} h(x) \,dx$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{4} h(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{4} h(x) \,dx$ is the area between the curve $y=h(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=0$ and $x=4$.

![The graph of function h has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = 0 and x = 4, shaded. The segment at the top of the shaded region starts at (0, 2), moves rightward to (2, 2), then moves upward to (4, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/69054f94eb58f61f7f5a7de5887f0ecdc1b2299d)

Since the curve is $\blueD{\text{above}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the area is $\blueD{\text{positive}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function h has the shaded region divided into a rectangle and a triangle.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0a339e46cef7d9a21b99305016c9bb18a776641e)

We have one rectangle with base $4$ and height $2$ and one triangle with base $2$ and height $1$:

$\underbrace{4\cdot 2}_{\text{rectangle}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{2\cdot 1}{2}}_{\text{triangle}}=9$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{4} h(x) \,dx=9$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $f$ is graphed.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 5, 4), moves rightward to (negative 4, 4), moves downward to (negative 1, 0), moves rightward to (1, 0), moves downward to (2, negative 2), moves upward and ends at (5, 1).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6fa490d23192af3d8065c620dc196950a6430e7a)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{-1} f(x) \,dx$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{-1} f(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{-1} f(x) \,dx$ is the area between the curve $y=f(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-5$ and $x=-1$.

![The graph of function f has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = negative 5 and x = negative 1, shaded. The segment at the top of the shaded region moves rightward from (negative 5, 4) to (negative 4, 4) then downward to (negative 1, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/063f975e42d3305ac48c80bce053acc7d1fdb77e)

Since the curve is $\blueD{\text{above}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the area is $\blueD{\text{positive}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function f has the shaded region divided into a rectangle and a triangle. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/036f5ddc20d09b927d99cb261509ae65cda0b8e6)

We have one rectangle with base $1$ and height $4$ and one triangle with base $3$ and height $4$:

$\underbrace{1\cdot 4}_{\text{rectangle}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{3\cdot 4}{2}}_{\text{triangle}}=10$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{-1} f(x) \,dx=10$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $g$ is graphed.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 5, 5), moves horizontally to the right to (negative 3, 5), moves downward to (negative 1, 1), continues downward to (1, 0), continues downward to (3, negative 4), moves upward and ends at (5, 0). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/73c89cf1708d70684bca9c4c2417cae9687aa716)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{1} g(x) \,dx$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{1} g(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{1} g(x) \,dx$ is the area between the curve $y=g(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-3$ and $x=1$.

![The graph of function g has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = negative 3 and x = 1, shaded. The segment at the top of the shaded region starts at (negative 3, 5), moves downward to (negative 1, 1), continues downward and ends at (1, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6e79776aa48d398283ad29fa7fb4f010390a17c)

Since the curve is $\blueD{\text{above}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the area is $\blueD{\text{positive}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function g has the shaded region divided into 2 triangles and a rectangle. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eba38b834d903ed8e00af35516b434382cd3ca9a)

We have one triangle with base $2$ and height $4$, one rectangle with base $2$ and height $1$, and one triangle with base $2$ and height $1$:

$\underbrace{\dfrac{4\cdot 2}{2}}_{\text{triangle #1}}+\underbrace{2\cdot 1}_{\text{rectangle}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{2\cdot 1}{2}}_{\text{triangle #2}}=7$

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{1} g(x) \,dx=7$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $f$ is graphed.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 5, 2), moves downward to (negative 2, negative 1), moves rightward to (1, negative 1), moves downward to (3, negative 4), moves upward and ends at (5, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ee544cafb4329edc6e8086338c3c791e4343b6ec)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{3} f(x) \,dx$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{3} f(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{3} f(x) \,dx$ is the net area between the curve $y=f(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-2$ and $x=3$.

![The graph of function f has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = negative 2 and x = 3, shaded. The segment at the bottom of the shaded region moves rightward from (negative 2, negative 1) to (1, negative 1) then downward to (3, negative 4). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7ba4d573f6d356227ff7a862130b7f9e3c299acd)

Since the curve is $\maroonD{\text{below}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the definite integral is $\maroonD{\text{negative}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function f has the shaded region divided into a rectangle and a triangle. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e3a4dc5377b554b6f2110223af791cc1d50d9575)

We have one rectangle with base $5$ and height $1$ and one triangle with base $2$ and height $3$:

$\underbrace{5\cdot 1}_{\text{rectangle}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{2\cdot 3}{2}}_{\text{triangle}}=8$

The total area is $8$ square units, so the integral is $-8$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{3} f(x) \,dx=-8$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $g$ is graphed.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 5, negative 1), moves downward to (negative 2, negative 4), moves rightward to (0, negative 4), moves upward to (2, 4), moves downward and ends at (5, 1). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e15c798a32104f904e747f001359082d239c4be7)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-4}^{-1} g(x) \,dx$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-4}^{-1} g(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
We can find this area using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function f has the shaded region divided into 2 rectangles and a triangle. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d54a57c9eb1ba4bc67814b4d1861ed059e6da863)

We have one rectangle with base $3$ and height $2$, one triangle with base $2$ and height $2$, and one rectangle with base $1$ and height $2$:

$\underbrace{3\cdot 2}_{\text{rectangle #1}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{2\cdot 2}{2}}_{\text{triangle}}+\underbrace{1\cdot 2}_{\text{rectangle #2}}=10$

The total area is $10$ square units, so the integral is $-10$.

$\displaystyle \int_{-4}^{-1} g(x) \,dx$ is the net area between the curve $y=g(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-4$ and $x=-1$.

![The graph of function g has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = negative 4 and x = negative 1, shaded. The segment at the bottom of the shaded region moves downward from (negative 4, negative 2) to (negative 2, negative 4) then moves rightward to (negative 1, negative 4). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/78bfc2f362ab4b2e2213b588914bdf893abf4fc5)

Since the curve is $\maroonD{\text{below}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the definite integral is $\maroonD{\text{negative}}$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-4}^{-1} g(x) \,dx=-10$

$\displaystyle \int_{2}^{5} h(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:

![The graph of function h has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = 2 and x = 5, shaded. The segment at the bottom of the shaded region moves rightward from (2, negative 2) to (3, negative 2), moves downward to (4, negative 4), then moves upward to (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cc622e4b955db5f9a2f7ecccd8eb25b619bf278b)

Since the curve is $\maroonD{\text{below}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the definite integral is $\maroonD{\text{negative}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function h has the shaded region divided into a rectangle and 2 triangles. ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bf54dd398cab9d2193487ae65560e22363ee27ee)

We have one rectangle with base $2$ and height $2$, one triangle with base $1$ and height $4$, and one triangle with base $1$ and height $2$:

$\underbrace{2\cdot 2}_{\text{rectangle}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{1\cdot 4}{2}}_{\text{triangle #1}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{1\cdot 2}{2}}_{\text{triangle #2}}=7$

The total area is $7$ square units, so the integral is $-7$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{2}^{5} h(x) \,dx=-7$

The set starts at (negative 5, 3), moves downward to (negative 3, negative 1), continues downward to (negative 1, negative 2), moves upward to (0, 0), moves downward to (1, negative 2), moves upward to (3, negative 1), continues upward and ends at (5, 3). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c2a86c6c7e49661da755d12f48e040ba0e3a5d89)

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{3} f(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:

![The graph of function f has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = 0 and x = 3, shaded. The segment at the bottom of the shaded region moves downward from (0, 0) to (1, negative 2) then upward to (3, negative 1). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a21d21588ef0a157f49a713230246c91a2c320d0)

Since the curve is $\maroonD{\text{below}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the definite integral is $\maroonD{\text{negative}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function f has the shaded region divided into a rectangle and 2 triangles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/09fc7e1ae4bbd874c2365cc0b0ae631fae75360f)

We have one triangle with base $1$ and height $2$, one rectangle with base $2$ and height $1$, and one triangle with base $2$ and height $1$:

$\underbrace{\dfrac{1\cdot 2}{2}}_{\text{triangle #1}}+\underbrace{2\cdot 1}_{\text{rectangle}}+\underbrace{\dfrac{2\cdot 1}{2}}_{\text{triangle #2}}=4$

The total area is $4$ square units, so the integral is $-4$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{3} f(x) \,dx=-4$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $h$ is graphed.

![Function h is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 5, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum at (negative 3, 2), moves downward through an inflection point at (negative 1, 0) to a relative minimum at (2, negative 3), moves upward and ends at (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/589657f1afe8bea812a76f152eb1f94af884ea0f)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{-1} h(x) \,dx$. Give an exact answer as a multiple of $\pi$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{-1} h(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{-1} h(x) \,dx$ is the net area between the curve $y=h(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-5$ and $x=-1$.

![The graph of function h has the region between the curve and the x-axis, between x = negative 5 and x = negative 1, shaded. The portion of the curve at the top of the shaded region is concave down, moving upward from (negative 5, 0) to (negative 3, 2) then downward to (negative 1, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8caeab46bcc323b382a2e8c02f64407a1300263f)

Since the curve is $\blueD{\text{above}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the area is $\blueD{\text{positive}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas. We have half a circle whose radius is $\goldD{2}$.

The area of the entire circle is $\pi\cdot \goldD{2}^2=4\pi$, and half of that is $\dfrac{4\pi}{2}=2\pi$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-5}^{-1} h(x) \,dx=2\pi$

assessment_items.question_content:

The curve starts at (negative 5, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum at (negative 4, 1), moves downward through an inflection point at (negative 3, 0) to a relative minimum at (negative 2, negative 1), moves upward through an inflection point at (negative 1, 0), continues upward to a relative maximum at (2, 3), moves downward and ends at (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ff8f216c808b22a1cbe0a102f4cc25aa51d0fcb7)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{5} f(x) \,dx$. Give an exact answer as a multiple of $\pi$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{5} f(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{5} f(x) \,dx$ is the net area between the curve $y=f(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-1$ and $x=5$.

![The graph of function f has the region between the curve and the x-axis, between x = negative 1 and x = 5, shaded. The portion of the curve at the top of the shaded region is concave down, moving upward from (negative 1, 0) to (2, 3) then downward to (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b536f84fa8b520b931a18fc351daa2ead3012dc9)

Since the curve is $\blueD{\text{above}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the area is $\blueD{\text{positive}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas. We have half a circle whose radius is $\goldD{3}$.

The area of the entire circle is $\pi\cdot \goldD{3}^2=9\pi$, and half of that is $\dfrac{9\pi}{2}$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-1}^{5} f(x) \,dx=\dfrac{9\pi}{2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $g$ is graphed.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 5, 0), moves downward to a relative minimum at about (negative 3.5, negative 1.5), moves upward through an inflection point at (negative 2, 0) to a relative maximum at (0, 2), moves downward through an inflection point at (2, 0), continues downward to a relative minimum at about (3.5, negative 1.5), moves upward and ends at (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d5227f44cdc0939f8f275c1a3ca5cbc272328aaa)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{2} g(x) \,dx$. Give an exact answer as a multiple of $\pi$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{2} g(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{2} g(x) \,dx$ is the net area between the curve $y=g(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=0$ and $x=2$.

![The graph of function g has the region between the curve and the x-axis, between x = 0 and x = 2, shaded. The portion of the curve at the top of the shaded region moves downward concave down from (0, 2) to (2, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/30ad1fbb6ccf98216c9896504ee9e5f8fe08f1bd)

Since the curve is $\blueD{\text{above}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the area is $\blueD{\text{positive}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas. We have a quarter of a circle whose radius is $\goldD{2}$.

The area of the entire circle is $\pi\cdot \goldD{2}^2=4\pi$, and a quarter of that is $\dfrac{4\pi}{4}=\pi$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{2} g(x) \,dx=\pi$

assessment_items.question_content:

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 5, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum at (negative 4, 1), moves downward through an inflection point at (negative 3, 0) to a relative minimum at (negative 2, negative 1), moves upward through an inflection point at (negative 1, 0) to a relative maximum at (1, 2), moves downward through an inflection point at (3, 0) to a relative minimum at (4, negative 1), moves upward and ends at (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7aa287feedbc1c984b28470d077d1c6559593be3)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{-1} f(x) \,dx$. Give an exact answer as a multiple of $\pi$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{-1} f(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{-1} f(x) \,dx$ is the net area between the curve $y=f(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-3$ and $x=-1$.

![The graph of function f has the region between the curve and the x-axis, between x = negative 3 and x = negative 1, shaded. The portion of the curve at the bottom of the shaded region moves downward from (negative 3, 0) to a relative minimum at (negative 2, negative 1) then moves upward to (negative 1, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9f171f35742cce2fa3ddd98f0628f4caccf0959f)

Since the curve is $\maroonD{\text{below}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the definite integral is $\maroonD{\text{negative}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas. We have half a circle whose radius is $\goldD{1}$.

The area of the entire circle is $\pi\cdot \goldD{1}^2=\pi$, and half of that is $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

The area is $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ square units, so the integral is $-\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-3}^{-1} f(x) \,dx=-\dfrac{\pi}{2}$

The area is $2\pi$ square units, so the integral is $-2\pi$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^{4} g(x) \,dx=-2\pi$

The curve starts at (negative 5, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum at about (negative 3.5, 1.5), moves downward through an inflection point at (negative 2, 0) to a relative minimum at (0, negative 2), moves upward through an inflection point at (2, 0), continues upward to a relative maximum at (3, 1), moves downward through an inflection point at (4, 0) to a relative minimum at about (4.5, negative 0.5), moves upward and ends at (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1b1a85f611ab17604b7197b0f46623e58b990fd4)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{0} h(x) \,dx$. Give an exact answer as a multiple of $\pi$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{0} h(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{0} h(x) \,dx$ is the net area between the curve $y=h(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=-2$ and $x=0$.

![The graph of function h has the region between the curve and the x-axis, between x = negative 2 and x = 0, shaded. The portion of the curve at the bottom of the shaded region moves downward concave up from (negative 2, 0) to (0, negative 2). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a918d285c085985a373917d92f6847645e792114)

Since the curve is $\maroonD{\text{below}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the definite integral is $\maroonD{\text{negative}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas. We have a quarter of a circle whose radius is $\goldD{2}$.

The area of the entire circle is $\pi\cdot \goldD{2}^2=4\pi$, and a quarter of that is $\dfrac{4\pi}{4}=\pi$.

The area is $\pi$ square units, so the integral is $-\pi$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{-2}^{0} h(x) \,dx=-\pi$

assessment_items.question_content:
Function $f$ is graphed.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph consists of a smooth curve. The curve starts at (negative 5, 0), moves upward to a relative maximum at (negative 3, 2), moves downward through inflection point (negative 1, 0), continues downward to relative minimum (2, negative 3), moves upward and ends at (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/66bf5787a9b2ba3116c6838c1aaf807a2bf49d3f)

**Find $\displaystyle \int_{2}^{5} f(x) \,dx$. Give an exact answer as a multiple of $\pi$.**

$\displaystyle \int_{2}^{5} f(x) \,dx=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
$\displaystyle \int_{2}^{5} f(x) \,dx$ is the net area between the curve $y=f(x)$ and the $x$-axis between $x=2$ and $x=5$.

![The graph of function f has the region between the curve and the x-axis, between x = 2 and x = 5, shaded. The portion of the curve at the bottom of the shaded region moves upward concave up from (2, negative 3) to (5, 0).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1a4adac89df17e33a2bd1072ccb6d22b74c81ac6)

Since the curve is $\maroonD{\text{below}}$ the $x$-axis in this interval, the definite integral is $\maroonD{\text{negative}}$.

We can find this area using geometric area formulas. We have a quarter of a circle whose radius is $\goldD{3}$.

The area of the entire circle is $\pi\cdot \goldD{3}^2=9\pi$, and a quarter of that is $\dfrac{9\pi}{4}$.

The area is $\dfrac{9\pi}{4}$ square units, so the integral is $-\dfrac{9\pi}{4}$.

In conclusion,

$\displaystyle \int_{2}^{5} f(x) \,dx=-\dfrac{9\pi}{4}$
